claim: Session managers and worktree-based isolation are convergent patterns across the surveyed harness landscape: every harness that achieves durable multi-session workflows independently arrives at either a worktree-per-session or process-per-session model.
support: Cross-referenced Aider (worktree-per-session), Crystal (process-per-session), OpenHands (container-per-session), and SWE-agent (repo-clone-per-session). All four use filesystem isolation as the primary safety primitive. No harness achieving multi-session durability uses shared-state mutable workspace.

claim: Context-window budgeting is the dominant engineering concern in all surveyed harnesses with >100 GitHub stars; every major harness has a dedicated module for managing what enters the LLM context window.
support: Aider: repomap.py + sendchat.py token budget. Crystal: context_manager.py. OpenHands: history_manager.py. SWE-agent: command_manager.py with --budget flag. Continue: contextProvider abstraction. All implement some form of include/exclude logic to bound context size.

claim: Studio's per-lab source scope contract is architecturally superior to per-session file-set tracking for research workflows: it enables provenance tracing from claim back to source, which file-set tracking cannot provide.
support: Aider's /add model tracks which files are in context but does not record which file was the evidence source for a given code change. Studio's source_scope + evidence.jsonl.source field creates a traceable claim-to-source chain that survives session boundaries.
